food_subgroup: Indo-Chinese deep-fried vegetable dish with sauce

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Indo-Chinese Veg Manchurian. A0FAN (mixed vegetable dishes, cooked) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Veg Manchurian or Indo-Chinese vegetable ball dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Veg Manchurian is approximately 250 g, comprising 6–8 vegetable balls either served dry with a light sauce coating or in a thick gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Manchurian (vegetable), cooked — estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 and USDA FoodData Central component data for deep-fried mixed vegetable preparations
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Veg Manchurian exists in ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017 or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for deep-fried mixed vegetable preparations (e.g., mixed vegetable cutlet, vegetable pakora) with USDA FoodData Central data for soy-sauce-based stir-fried vegetable dishes. The dish is a deep-fried vegetable ball (cabbage, carrot, beans, cornflour binder) tossed in a soy-chilli-ginger sauce. Energy and macros reflect the combined contribution of the fried batter-coated balls and the sauce. Values represent a mid-range estimate for the dry/gravy variant as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable and flour-based deep-fried preparations — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable fritters, battered/fried vegetable dishes, and cornflour-thickened sauces
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Veg Manchurian. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogues: deep-fried vegetable fritters/pakoras (GI ~50–60), cornflour-thickened sauces (GI ~55–65), and mixed vegetable dishes with refined flour binder. The dominant glycemic drivers are the cornflour and all-purpose flour binder in the vegetable balls and the cornflour-thickened sauce. GI 55 falls within the 'low' category (55 or less). GL per serving = (55 × 19.5 × 250/100) / 100 = 26.8, rounded to 26.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg/100 g) from soy sauce, chilli sauce, and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Veg Manchurian due to its high sodium content (~1300 mg per 250 g serving), which represents more than 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting a reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side option can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) and moderate carbohydrate load from refined flour binder and cornflour-thickened sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Veg Manchurian in moderation; the low GI (~55) and glycemic load (~27 per serving) from the refined flour and cornflour components can cause a moderate postprandial glucose rise, and portion control to half a standard serving is advisable when consumed as part of a mixed meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content from deep-frying and elevated sodium from soy-based sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Veg Manchurian due to its combined burden of deep-frying-derived fat (~8.5 g fat/100 g) and high sodium (~520 mg/100 g); the dish is cholesterol-free and uses vegetable oil, which partially mitigates cardiovascular risk, but the sodium and total fat load warrant portion restriction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Manchurian is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion dish originating in India, widely served across Indian restaurants in Singapore and globally. It consists of small balls made from finely chopped or grated mixed vegetables — typically cabbage, carrot, French beans, and capsicum — bound with cornflour and all-purpose flour, seasoned with ginger, garlic, and green chilli, then deep-fried until golden and crisp. The fried balls are then tossed in a glossy, umami-rich sauce made from soy sauce, tomato ketchup, chilli sauce, vinegar, ginger, garlic, and spring onion, thickened with cornflour. The dish is served in two styles: 'dry', where the balls are lightly coated with sauce and retain their crispness, and 'gravy', where the balls are submerged in a generous amount of the sauce. The flavour profile is savoury, tangy, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet, with a crispy exterior giving way to a soft vegetable interior. Nutritionally, Veg Manchurian provides approximately 165 kcal per 100 g, with moderate carbohydrates (~19.5 g) from the flour binder and sauce, moderate fat (~8.5 g) from deep-frying, and modest protein (~3.8 g) from the vegetable and flour components. The dish is high in sodium (~520 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce and chilli sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its low glycemic index (~55) and moderate glycemic load (~27 per 250 g serving) require portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is fully vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, and is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Indian-Chinese restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Veg Manchurian?
Is Veg Manchurian suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between dry and gravy Manchurian?

Can I eat Veg Manchurian if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Veg Manchurian exists in ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017 or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from analogous deep-fried mixed vegetable preparations and soy-sauce-based stir-fried dishes from approved sources. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar flour-bound deep-fried vegetable dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish has a well-defined and consistent preparation method, the component ingredients are well-characterised in approved sources, and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017 and USDA FoodData Central analogues (deep-fried mixed vegetable preparations, soy-sauce-based dishes); no exact database entry exists for Veg Manchurian. 2. Values represent a mid-range estimate applicable to both dry and gravy variants; the gravy variant may have slightly higher sodium and carbohydrate due to additional sauce volume. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source entries used for estimation. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero by formulation. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this dish contains no animal-derived or fortified ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; confirmed zero by formulation. 7. GI is estimated at 55 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogues for deep-fried vegetable fritters and cornflour-thickened preparations; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 8. GL per 250 g serving = (55 × 19.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 26.8, reported as 26.9. 9. The dish is served at Curry Village – Authentic North Indian Cuisine (Boat Quay, Singapore) via field survey at SGD 16.90. 10. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact ontology term exists for Indo-Chinese Veg Manchurian.
